medications.drug_name: no medication to be initiated via telehealth in this encounter
medications.indication_rationale: Rate control (e.g. beta-blocker) and anticoagulation (e.g. DOAC) are anticipated components of AF management, but must be initiated by the assessing clinician after ECG confirmation, stroke risk (CHA2DS2-VASc) and bleeding risk (HAS-BLED) scoring, and review of renal function — not prescribed sight-unseen via telehealth chat. The source note does not specify a dose for any such medication.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest and avoid strenuous physical exertion until seen and assessed in person today.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces symptomatic burden and myocardial demand while haemodynamic stability and rhythm are confirmed.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid caffeine and alcohol until reviewed, as both can act as AF triggers or exacerbate symptoms.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Caffeine and alcohol are recognised sympathomimetic/arrhythmogenic triggers that can worsen palpitation burden.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Arrange for someone to check in on you today given you live alone, or keep your phone close by, in case symptoms worsen before your in-person review.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient lives alone and has reported feeling vulnerable due to lightheadedness; ensuring a safety check-in reduces risk if symptoms progress before she is seen.

patient_education_points.point: The top chambers of your heart appear to be beating in a fast, disorganised way instead of a steady rhythm, which is why your pulse feels irregular and you feel washed out — this is called atrial fibrillation, and we need an ECG (heart tracing) to confirm it.

You need to be seen in person today for an ECG and examination — either an urgent GP appointment with same-day ECG access, or urgent care, so we can confirm the rhythm and check how your heart is coping.

Because this heart rhythm can increase the risk of stroke, and you're not currently on any blood-thinning medication, the clinician who sees you today will also assess your stroke risk and likely start medication to reduce that risk, alongside medication to help control your heart rate.

Since you live alone, please try to have someone check on you today, or keep your phone within reach, until you've been assessed in person — if you feel worse, faint, or develop chest pain or any stroke-like symptoms (face drooping, slurred speech, arm or leg weakness), call 000 immediately.
Avoiding caffeine and alcohol and resting quietly until you're seen can help reduce how noticeable the palpitations feel, though the underlying rhythm still needs proper assessment and treatment.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same day — urgent in-person medical review with ECG access today
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: 12-lead ECG confirmation of rhythm, rate control adequacy, CHA2DS2-VASc stroke risk score, HAS-BLED bleeding risk score, thyroid function and other reversible-cause screening, and anticoagulation initiation
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Chest pain, worsening shortness of breath, syncope, or any stroke-like symptoms (facial droop, slurred speech, one-sided weakness) — call 000 immediately rather than waiting

must_include_items: Recommendation for urgent same-day in-person assessment with ECG to confirm the rhythm
Explicit mention that this heart rhythm carries a stroke risk and that stroke-risk assessment/anticoagulation will need to be addressed
Safety-netting for stroke symptoms (facial droop, slurred speech, limb weakness) and chest pain — call 000
Acknowledgement of the patient living alone and a practical safety step (check-in, phone within reach) pending in-person review
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending direct presentation to the emergency department rather than urgent GP/urgent care is an acceptable alternative escalation route
Framing the working diagnosis as 'an irregular heart rhythm' or 'possible atrial fibrillation' rather than stating it as fully confirmed is appropriate given telehealth limits
errors_of_omission: No recommendation for urgent same-day ECG/in-person assessment
No mention of stroke risk or anticoagulation despite new AF and stroke risk factors
No acknowledgement of the patient's social isolation as a safety consideration
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient to simply rest at home and follow up in a week without urgent ECG
Prescribing or naming a specific rate-control or anticoagulant medication and dose via telehealth without in-person assessment
Treating this as a purely symptomatic issue without recognising the mandatory stroke risk stratification component
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an important-not-to-miss case due to stroke risk. Any response that fails to include both (1) urgent same-day in-person ECG-capable assessment AND (2) explicit acknowledgement of stroke risk requiring further work-up is a significant safety gap on the management domain.
